title: Kubernetes Core Concepts for Developers
excerpt: Learn Kubernetes from a developer perspective: pods, clusters, deployments, and why it matters beyond DevOps.

body.value:
Kubernetes (K8s) is an open-source platform designed to automate the deployment, scaling, and management of containerized applications.

Instead of manually managing containers across multiple machines, Kubernetes acts as an orchestration layer that ensures your application is always running in the desired state.

value.title: Key Idea
value.text: Kubernetes is like a conductor of an orchestra — it ensures all containers (musicians) work together correctly.

value.caption: Kubernetes as a container orchestrator
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    Dev[Developer] --> K8s[Kubernetes Cluster]
    K8s --> Node1[Worker Node]
    K8s --> Node2[Worker Node]
    Node1 --> Pod1[Pod]
    Node2 --> Pod2[Pod]

body.value:
Many developers assume Kubernetes is only for DevOps teams. However, understanding Kubernetes provides major advantages:

  • Run production-like environments locally
  • Test scalability and resilience
  • Debug real deployment issues
  • Work effectively with DevOps teams

value.title: Real Benefit
value.text: With Kubernetes, your app can scale, heal itself, and deploy without downtime — automatically.

body.value:
A Kubernetes cluster consists of two main components:

  • Master Node – controls the system
  • Worker Nodes – run your applications

The master node receives instructions and schedules workloads across worker nodes.

value.caption: Cluster architecture
value.diagram:
flowchart TB
    User --> API[kubectl / API Server]
    API --> Master[Master Node]
    Master --> Worker1[Worker Node]
    Master --> Worker2[Worker Node]
    Worker1 --> Pod1
    Worker2 --> Pod2

body.value:
A Pod is the smallest deployable unit in Kubernetes. It acts as a wrapper around one or more containers.

In most cases, a Pod contains a single container, but multiple tightly coupled containers can share the same Pod.

value.title: Important
value.text: Pods are ephemeral — if one dies, Kubernetes replaces it instead of restarting it.

value.caption: Pod structure
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    Pod --> Container1
    Pod --> Container2

value.code:
kubectl get pods
kubectl run my-nginx --image=nginx:alpine
kubectl port-forward my-nginx 8080:80
kubectl delete pod my-nginx
value.caption: Essential kubectl commands

body.value: kubectl is the main CLI tool used to interact with Kubernetes. It communicates with the cluster's API server and allows you to manage resources.

• Self-healing – replaces failed containers automatically
  • Scaling – increase or decrease instances easily
  • Zero-downtime deployments – update apps without downtime
  • Service discovery – automatic networking between components
  • Configuration management – using ConfigMaps and Secrets

value.caption: Self-healing example
value.diagram:
flowchart LR
    Pod1[Running Pod] --> Crash[Crash]
    Crash --> K8s[Kubernetes]
    K8s --> NewPod[New Pod Created]

body.value:
Kubernetes supports two approaches:

  • Imperative – run commands directly using kubectl
  • Declarative – define desired state in YAML files

value.code:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: my-nginx
spec:
  containers:
    - name: nginx
      image: nginx:alpine
value.caption: Example Pod YAML definition

value.title: Best Practice
value.text: Always prefer declarative YAML files for production environments.

body.value:
Kubernetes is not just a DevOps tool — it is a core platform for modern application development.

  • Pods are the foundation
  • Clusters manage distributed workloads
  • kubectl is your control interface
  • YAML defines desired state

Understanding these concepts allows you to build scalable, resilient applications.
